title: Chapter VII.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: But (as we had begun to remark) it is not sufficient for a Christian to keep himself from wickedness, unless he also has fulfilled the duties implied in good works, as is very distinctly proved by that statement in which the Lord threatened that those will be doomed to eternal fire, who, although they have done no evil, have not done all that is good, declaring, “Then will the king say to those who are on his right hand: depart from me, ye cursed, into eternal fire, which my Father has prepared for the devil and his angels; for I was hungry, and ye gave me not to eat; I was thirsty, and ye gave me no drink,” with what follows.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: He did not say, “Depart from me, ye cursed, because ye have committed murder, or adultery, or theft”; for it is not because they had done evil, but because they had not done good, that they are condemned, and doomed to the punishments of the eternal Gehenna; nor because they had committed things which were forbidden, but because they had not been willing to do those things which had been commanded.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: And from this it is to be observed what hope those can have, who, in addition, do some of those things which are forbidden, when even such are doomed to eternal fire as have simply not done the things which are commanded.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: For I do not wish you to flatter yourself in this way,—if you have not done certain things, because you have done certain other things, since it is written, “Whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, has become guilty of all.”

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: For Adam sinned once, and died; and do you think that you can live, when you are often doing that which killed another person, when he had only done it once?

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: Or do you imagine that he committed a great crime, and was therefore justly condemned to a severer punishment?

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: Let us consider, then, what it was he really did.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: He ate of the fruit of the tree, contrary to the commandment.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: Did God punish man with death for the sake of the fruit of a tree?

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: No: not on account of the fruit of the tree, but on account of the contempt of the commandment.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: The question, therefore, is not about the nature of the offense, but about the transgression of the commandment.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: And the same being who told Adam not to eat of the fruit of the tree, has commanded you not to speak evil, not to lie, not to detract, not to listen to a detractor, to swear not at all, not to covet, not to envy, not to be drunken, not to be greedy, not to render evil for evil to any one, to love your enemies, to bless them that curse you, to pray for them that malign and persecute you, to turn the other cheek to one smiting you, and not to go to law before a worldly tribunal, so that, if any one seeks to take away your goods, you should joyfully lose them, to flee from the charge of avarice, to beware of the sin of all pride and boastfulness, and live, humble and meek, after the example of Christ, avoiding fellowship with the wicked so completely that you will not even eat with fornicators, or covetous persons, or those that speak evil of others, or the envious, or detractors, or the drunken, or the rapacious.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: Now, if you despise him in any such matter, then, if he spared Adam, he will also spare you.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: Yea, he might have been spared with better reason than you, inasmuch as he was still ignorant and inexperienced, and was restrained by the example of no one who had previously sinned, and who had died on account of his sin.

verses.book: Letter II. A Letter of Sulpitius Severus to His Sister Claudia Concerning Virginity.
verses.text: But after such examples as you possess, after the law, after the prophets, after the gospels, and after the apostles, if you still set your mind on transgressing, I see not in what way pardon can be extended to you.
